re_check_of: prior FAIL on forward_reference_drift (this file, passed=false, at=2026-07-14T00:00:00Z)
fixes_verified: Section 5.4 generator worked example rewritten: `first_five = [next(fib) for _ in range(5)]` replaced with a plain counter + `for` loop + `break` pattern (`count = 0`; `for value in fibonacci(): print(value); count += 1; if count == 5: break`). No list-comprehension syntax, no bare `next()` call remains anywhere in the corpus.

Section 6 Implementation, 'Building a lazy generator' step 4 trimmed to: 'Consume the generator with a `for` loop, using a counter and `break` if the generator is unbounded.' No mention of `next()` or `list(...)` remains.

required_sections.detail: Section 3 Learning Objectives (5 non-trivial bullets), Section 4 Introduction (~170 words, >=50 word floor), Section 5 Core Concepts (4 sub-concepts 5.1-5.4, well over 100-word floor), Section 10 Key Takeaways (5 substantive bullets) all present and non-trivial. Unaffected by the two fixes. Section 11 Next Steps carries the system-derived placeholder, which is acceptable since Next Steps is not a required-non-trivial section.

citation_density.detail: Every resource in topic.resources.json is still cited: [1]=realpython lambda (primary, cited ~7x), [2]=docs.python.org functional HOWTO (supporting, cited ~5x), [3]=realpython decorators (supporting, cited ~4x), [4]=realpython generators (supporting, cited ~5x). Sole primary resource cited -- satisfies the hard rule. No fabricated [N] markers. Section 5's rewritten 5.4 consumption example does not introduce any new uncited factual claim -- the citation-bearing sentences in 5.4 (definition of generator function [4], lazy-evaluation definition [2][4]) are unchanged; only the code sample and one procedural sentence describing it were edited, and that procedural sentence is not a standalone factual assertion requiring its own marker (same treatment as the rest of the worked-example walkthrough prose). Section 6 remains uncited but is exclusively procedural how-to steps restating already-cited 5.2/5.3/5.4 concepts, consistent with the prior pass's reasoning. Combined 5+7 density clears the 60% floor for standalone factual assertions.

prior_concepts_checked.1.x_2.x: concepts_introduced arrays are empty (not yet corpus-drafted); evident subject matter (variables, operators, statements, conditionals, loops) treated as fair-game baseline vocabulary, consistent with the prior critic pass.

targeted_recheck.section_5.4: Full-text scan confirms no `next(`, no `[... for ... in ...]` comprehension syntax, no `__iter__`/`__next__`/`StopIteration`/'iterator protocol' anywhere in the section. The Fibonacci-consumption example now uses only previously-taught constructs: `for` loop, plain variable increment, `if`/`==` comparison, and `break` (all baseline 2.1/2.2 vocabulary already used elsewhere in this same corpus without objection).
targeted_recheck.section_6: Step 4 of the 'Building a lazy generator' recipe now reads only in terms of `for` loop + counter + `break` -- matches the syntax actually taught and demonstrated in 5.4. No remaining reference to `next()` or `list(...)`.

whole_corpus_rescan.method: Grep for next\(|comprehension|__iter__|__next__|StopIteration|iterator protocol across the full topic_corpus.md, plus manual re-read of every code block and every sentence in sections 5-9 for other unexplained syntax.
whole_corpus_rescan.result: Only one hit for 'comprehension': Section 5.2 closing paragraph -- 'a list comprehension (a later topic) often reads more clearly for the same job.' This is a named, explicitly-flagged-as-future preview reference (not used as unexplained syntax in any code sample anywhere in the corpus). Judged as acceptable pedagogical forward-pointer language, not a forward-reference violation: it does not require the reader to understand comprehension syntax to follow the current sentence, and it explicitly signals the construct is not yet covered -- the same category of forward-pointer commonly used across curricula ('you'll see X in a later topic'). This is distinct from the previously-failing defect, which was unexplained syntax *used* in a worked example.
other_candidates_checked_and_cleared: `import time` / `time.time()` (5.3 timer example, also present in Introduction) -- stdlib import boilerplate, not a taught 'concept' of this topic or 3.1; treated as baseline tooling vocabulary consistent with the prior pass (not flagged there either) rather than a core-concept forward reference.

Tuple assignment / simultaneous swap `a, b = 0, 1` / `a, b = b, a + b` (5.4 Fibonacci) -- basic variable-assignment idiom, baseline 1.2 vocabulary, unchanged from the previously-reviewed version and not newly introduced by the fixes.
f-strings and format specs (`f"{func.__name__} took {end - start:.4f} seconds"`, 5.3) -- baseline output vocabulary, unchanged from the previously-reviewed version, not flagged in the prior pass.
Underscore throwaway loop variable `for _ in range(10_000_000):` (5.3 timer example) -- ordinary `for`/`range()` loop, baseline 2.2 vocabulary; unchanged from the previously-reviewed version.

forward_reference_drift.detail: Both defects identified in the prior FAIL are confirmed fixed: no unexplained list-comprehension syntax and no bare `next()` call remain anywhere in the corpus (section 5.4's worked example and section 6 step 4 both now use only previously-taught `for`/counter/`break` constructs). The self-reported claim that iterator-protocol vocabulary (`__iter__`/`__next__`/`StopIteration`) was avoided remains independently confirmed true. The sole remaining borderline item -- the named, parenthetical 'list comprehension (a later topic)' mention in 5.2 -- is judged non-blocking preview language, not a syntax-level forward reference, and is left as-is rather than requiring further edits.

length_sanity.reference_range_used: artifact-mix.json profiles.deep.reading.target_word_range = [1800, 2200] (per task-specified authoritative source, same as prior check)
length_sanity.method: Manual whitespace-token recount of the full corpus body (excludes YAML frontmatter), matching the same split()-style methodology implied by the corpus-builder's prior self-report, cross-checked against the previous self-reported figure (2395) for order-of-magnitude consistency.
length_sanity.detail: Word count moved from 2395 (pre-fix) to ~2459 (post-fix) -- the two edits were a net wash-to-slight-increase (the section 5.4 rewrite added a short but explicit counter/`for`/`break` consumption block and surrounding prose, more than offsetting the words trimmed from section 6 step 4). 2459 is ~259 words / ~11.8% over the 2200 upper bound of the deep target range. This is a larger overage than the previous 8.9%, but still well inside the 30%-outside-range hard-fail tolerance the skill specifies (2200 * 1.3 = 2860; 2459 is nowhere near that ceiling). Soft-flagging again, non-blocking: recommend trimming ~250-300 words on a future pass if this corpus is revisited (candidates unchanged from before: the 5.2 map/filter closing paragraph, the 5.3 three-piece decorator-anatomy paragraph, plus now also the longer 5.4 Fibonacci-consumption walkthrough prose), but this does not block the current pass.

verdict: PASS (re-check, corrected). Both fixes from the prior FAIL are verified in place: section 5.4's generator example now uses a plain for-loop/counter/break pattern with no list-comprehension syntax and no bare next() call, and section 6's Implementation step 4 was trimmed to match, referencing only the for/counter/break pattern actually taught. A full whole-corpus rescan (regex + manual re-read of every code block and section 5-9 sentence) found no other occurrence of next(), comprehension syntax used in code, __iter__/__next__/StopIteration, or other unexplained core-concept constructs. The single remaining item -- a named, explicitly-flagged-as-future mention of 'list comprehension (a later topic)' in section 5.2's closing paragraph, not used as executable syntax anywhere -- is judged acceptable pedagogical preview language rather than a forward-reference violation, and does not block. Required sections, strict web_search citation density (all resources incl. sole primary cited), and length (recounted ~2459 words vs. deep target 1800-2200, ~11.8% over upper bound, well inside the 30% hard-fail tolerance) all pass, with length flagged again as an explicit non-blocking soft finding. No further changes required before this corpus proceeds to the next gate.
